1: theme Praise the LORD! For it is good to sing praises to our God; for it is pleasant, and a song of praise is fitting. Praise fitting and pleasant — the worship of the assembled church. Hebrews 13:15: through him then let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God, that is, the fruit of lips that acknowledge his name. Christ himself leads this praise (Hebrews 2:12; Psalms 22:22) — the fitting song is fitting because the Son sings it with his people.
2: shadow The LORD builds up Jerusalem; he gathers the outcasts of Israel. Building Jerusalem and gathering outcasts — the eschatological city. Matthew 23:37: O Jerusalem, Jerusalem... How often would I have gathered your children together as a hen gathers her brood under her wings. Revelation 21:2: the New Jerusalem coming down from heaven — the city the LORD builds in Christ as the gathering place of all his scattered people.
3: shadow He heals the brokenhearted and binds up their wounds. Healing the brokenhearted — Christ's Nazareth manifesto: The Spirit of the Lord is upon me, because he has anointed me to proclaim good news to the poor... to bind up the brokenhearted (Luke 4:18; Isaiah 61:1). The physician of Psalms 147:3 is the physician who comes to heal the sick (Matthew 9:12-13), entering the wreckage of human hearts to bind and restore.
4: revelation of God He determines the number of the stars; he gives to all of them their names. Naming all the stars — the Creator Word. John 1:3: All things were made through him, and without him was not any thing made that was made. Colossians 1:16-17: by him all things were created... and in him all things hold together. The one who names every star became the son of Mary, entering the creation he himself named, calling each person also by name (John 10:3).
5: revelation of God Great is our Lord, and abundant in power; his understanding is beyond measure. Power and understanding beyond measure — Christ in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge (Colossians 2:3). Ephesians 3:18-19: the breadth and length and height and depth of the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge. The immeasurable greatness of God's power is the same power that raised Christ from the dead (Ephesians 1:19-20).
6: shadow The LORD lifts up the humble; he casts the wicked to the ground. Lifting the humble and casting the wicked — the Magnificat: he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate (Luke 1:52). Matthew 23:12: whoever humbles himself will be exalted. The cross is the supreme act of this reversal: the humbled Servant (Philippians 2:8) is exalted above all (Philippians 2:9-11).
7: theme Sing to the LORD with thanksgiving; make melody to our God on the lyre! Thanksgiving and melody to God — the music of the new covenant church. Colossians 3:16: singing psalms and hymns and spiritual songs, with thankfulness in your hearts to God. Revelation 5:8: the four living creatures and the elders hold harps and sing to the Lamb. The lyre of Ps 147 becomes the harp of Revelation, played before the enthroned Lamb.
8: shadow He covers the heavens with clouds; he prepares rain for the earth; he makes grass grow on the hills. Providential care for the earth — the common grace Christ explains: he makes his sun rise on the evil and on the good, and sends rain on the just and on the unjust (Matthew 5:45). The God who covers heaven with clouds and grows grass is the Father Christ reveals — whose providence extends to every sparrow and every blade of grass (Matthew 6:26-30).
9: shadow He gives to the beasts their food, and to the young ravens that cry. Feeding beasts and ravens — the providence Christ cites: Look at the birds of the air: they neither sow nor reap nor gather into barns, and yet your heavenly Father feeds them. Are you not of more value than they? (Matthew 6:26). The God of Psalms 147:9 is the Father Christ reveals; if he feeds ravens, how much more will he provide for those who are in Christ?
10: shadow His delight is not in the strength of the horse, nor his pleasure in the legs of a man; God not delighting in human strength — the reversal of worldly power. 1 Corinthians 1:26-27: not many of you were wise according to worldly standards, not many were powerful, not many were of noble birth. But God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise. The cross is the supreme demonstration that God does not work through human strength but through the weakness of Christ (1 Corinthians 1:25; 2 Corinthians 13:4).
11: revelation of God but the LORD takes pleasure in those who fear him, in those who hope in his steadfast love. God's pleasure in those who fear and hope — the Father's pleasure in Christ: This is my beloved Son, with whom I am well pleased (Matthew 3:17; Psalms 17:5). Those in Christ share his relationship with the Father: the Father himself loves you, because you have loved me and have believed that I came from God (John 16:27). The pleasure of Psalms 147:11 is given to all who hope in God's steadfast love through Christ.
12: shadow Praise the LORD, O Jerusalem! Praise your God, O Zion! Jerusalem and Zion praising God — the eschatological city. Galatians 4:26: the Jerusalem above is free, and she is our mother. Hebrews 12:22: you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem. The praise of Zion is now the praise of the church that has come to the heavenly Zion through Christ.
13: shadow For he strengthens the bars of your gates; he blesses your children within you. Strengthened gates and blessed children — Matthew 16:18: the gates of hell shall not prevail against it. Christ strengthens the gates of his church against every assault. The blessed children within are those born of God (John 1:12-13), the children of Abraham by faith (Galatians 3:29), blessed in Christ with every spiritual blessing (Ephesians 1:3).
14: shadow He makes peace in your borders and fills you with the finest of the wheat. Peace and finest wheat — the Eucharist as the finest wheat. Christ: I am the bread of life; whoever comes to me shall not hunger (John 6:35). John 14:27: Peace I leave with you; my peace I give to you. The finest wheat of Psalms 147:14 is fulfilled in the bread of the Lord's Supper — the one who is both the peace-maker and the bread of life.
15: direct He sends out his command to the earth; his word runs swiftly. God sending his word swiftly — the Incarnation as the supreme sending of the Word. John 1:14: the Word became flesh and dwelt among us. 2 Timothy 2:9: the word of God is not bound. The word that runs swiftly to the ends of the earth is the gospel — the word made flesh in Jesus Christ, sent out through the apostles to every nation (Matthew 28:19-20).
16: shadow He gives snow like wool; he scatters frost like ashes; Snow and frost as God's word-command — the creative word of God that controls all creation. John 1:3: All things were made through him. Christ commands creation directly — he rebukes the storm (Mark 4:39), walks on water (Matthew 14:25). The one who commands snow and frost is the one who commands wind and wave to be still — the same creative Word at work.
17: shadow he hurls down his crystals of ice like crumbs; who can stand before his cold? Ice as God's creative power — the rhetorical question: who can stand before his cold? The implied answer is no one except the Word through whom all things were made. John 1:10: He was in the world, and the world was made through him, yet the world did not know him. The creator of ice entered his own creation as the child of Bethlehem.
18: shadow He sends out his word and melts them; he makes his wind blow and the waters flow. The word that melts ice and makes wind blow — the same word Christ speaks to nature. When he calms the storm (Mark 4:39 — Peace! Be still!), he is the Creator speaking to his creation. When he breathes on the disciples (John 20:22 — Receive the Holy Spirit), the wind of God flows through the risen Christ to make waters of life flow.
19: shadow He declares his word to Jacob, his statutes and rules to Israel. God declaring his word to Israel — the privilege that points to the crisis of the Incarnation. John 1:11: He came to his own, and his own people did not receive him. The word declared to Jacob and Israel is now the Word embodied in Jesus of Nazareth, the fulfillment of every statute and rule he once declared to his covenant people.
20: shadow He has not dealt thus with any other nation; they do not know his rules. Praise the LORD! Israel's unique privilege now opened to all nations through Christ — Ephesians 2:12-13: you Gentiles in the flesh... remember that you were... having no hope and without God in the world. But now in Christ Jesus you who once were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ. The nations that did not know God's rules now receive them through the gospel of Christ (Romans 15:9-12).
